items.title: Syria: How to find and get justice for Assad's victims? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Russian state media say that ousted Syrian leader Bashar al-Assad has been granted asylum in Moscow after fleeing his country. His regime collapsed in a lightning rebel offensive that began less than two weeks ago. Assad's fall has been a cause for widespread celebration across the country and among the Syrian diaspora. After almost 25 years under his rule, Syrians are beginning to turn their attention towards what rebel leaders say is a 'new era' for their homeland.
As thousands of victims are released from Assad's brutal prison system, difficulty remains in finding and releasing others, many of whom are underground in coded cells. How can justice be served with Assad in Russia?

00:00 Assad in Russia
00:27 DW's Gasia Ohanes reports from the studio in Berlin
06:01 DW speaks with Bente Scheller, Head of the Middle East and North Africa Division at the Heinrich Böll Foundation

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Newly restored Notre Dame cathedral held its first public mass | DW Shorts
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Around 2,500 believers attended Notre Dame's first public mass after its five-year restoration following a devastating fire. The event followed inauguration ceremonies over the weekend for special guests including French President Macron and US President-elect Donald Trump.

items.title: How the HTS group could impact Syria's future | DW Shorts
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Hayat Tahrir al-Sham, or HTS, has emerged as a key player in rebel forces toppling Assad's regime. The group has a militant Islamist background but has struck a more moderate tone in recent days. So how could it shape Syria's future? Middle East expert Haid Haid from the international affairs think tank Chatham House says whether HTS is willing to share powers and accept other democratic forces remains to be seen.

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Romanians split over constitutional court decision on presidential election | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Romania is now heading into a period of uncertainty following the annulment of last week's presidential elections. The country's constitutional court voided the result of the first round over concerns of foreign interference. The incumbent President Klaus Iohannis will remain in his post while the courts and the government look for ways to prevent any meddling when the election is held again. DW’s Jack Parrock has been speaking to people about the fallout of the ongoing political turmoil.  

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Friedrich Merz visits President Zelenskyy amidst German election race | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
The leader of Germany’s conservative opposition party CDU/CSU Friedrich Merz has visited the Ukrainian capital of Kyiv. Merz stands a good chance of becoming Germany's new leader after the upcoming snap elections in February. He met with Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy. 

We asked DW Chief Political Editor Michaela Küfner what Merz is hoping to achieve with his trip to war-torn Ukraine.


For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Germany suspends decisions on asylum applications by Syrians | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Syrians who fled the civil war in their country are spread out across the world. Germany hosts the largest Syrian community in Europe. Many Syrians are now flooding the streets of their adopted country to celebrate the sudden and stunning end to the Assad family's rule. The German government has voiced its solidarity with the diaspora — and announced that it's freezing all new asylum applications from Syrians.  

DW reporter Thomas Sparrow explains.


For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Humanitarian situation in Syria remains dire | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
The fall of the Assad regime has many Syrian refugees in Turkey hoping they'll be able to return home soon. The UN's refugee agency says Turkey has registered around 3.2 million Syrians. A day after rebels seized control of Damascus, Syrian refugees are lining up at border crossings. 
UNICEF says around 90 per cent of families in Syria are living in poverty, with more than 16 million people dependent on aid.

0:00 Syrian refugees and Syrians within the country are in need of humanitarian aid
 
0:32 Rasha Muhrez, Syria Response Director, Save the Children

3:30 Gonzalo Vatgas Llosa, UN Refugee Agency's representative for Syria

items.title: Burning devils is a tradition during the holiday season in Guatemala | DW Shorts
items.author: DW News
items.content:
In Guatemala, it is traditional to burn figures of devils made of cardboard, wire and wood. This is seen as a sign of purification to drive out evil. This centuries-old tradition marks the start of the Christmas season in Guatemala.

items.title: Rebels free Syrians from Saydnaya prison | DW Shorts
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Syrian rebels released people from Saydnaya prison near Damascus. Thousands of prisoners spilled out of Assad's detention system after his fall on Sunday, December 8. Families went through Saydnaya's complex in hope of finding missing loved ones. But by Monday afternoon there was no sign of more prisoners, after a rescue team searched for hidden underground cells.

items.title: Assad regime imprisoned hundreds of thousands in Syria | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Under now-ousted Syrian President Bashar al-Assad, tens of thousands of Syrians disappeared into the country's network of prisons. The slightest suspicion of dissent could result in incarceration. For many, it would also mean torture and death.

00:00 Assad regime imprisoned hundreds of thousands in Syria
02:09 Anwar al-Bunni, Syrian human rights lawyer AND Ibrahim al-Assil, Middle East Institute
15:15 Omar Alshogre, Human rights advocate

items.title: Man arrested over killing of healthcare executive Brian Thompson | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Police in the US state of Pennsylvania have arrested a suspect in the killing of United Healthcare CEO Brian Thompson. New York police said the man was spotted at a McDonald's near the town of Altoona. He had a firearm with a silencer and fraudulent identification matching the one used by the suspect to check into a New York hostel.

items.title: How will the security situation in Syria pan out? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Rebel leaders in Syria have begun to assert their control, closely watched by the international community. The head of the so-called joint 'Military Operations Command,' Abu Mohammed al-Jolani has dropped the nom de guerre associated with his jihadist past and is now using his real name: Ahmed al-Shara. He's trying to bolster his legitimacy by presenting a more moderate public image. Syria's parliament says it supports the people's will to build a new future. What that will look like remains unclear, but many Syrians say it can only get better. 

00:00 What's next for Syria?
03:21 Haid Haid, Chatham House
08:42 Andrew Sinclair, Center for Global Security and Stabilization
16:14 Steven Heydemann, Smith College
21:13 Joseph Bahout, Institute for Public and International Affairs
